disclaimer: Service impacts are educational estimates based on adopted budget documents. Actual service changes require City Council action.

library.unitLabel: library branch week of staffed hours

levels.label: Minimal
levels.description: Severe cuts to hours and programming; self-service only at most locations.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Reduced programming, fewer digital subscriptions, limited community events.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Current adopted service: five locations including Gateway Library; standard hours Mon–Sat.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Extended hours, expanded literacy and workforce programs, more staffing.

levels.label: Expanded
levels.description: Maximum programming, additional outreach, enhanced makerspace (THINKspot) access.

details: A 25%+ reduction would force difficult choices across five library locations.
Real FY 2025/26 cuts eliminated page positions and Press Reader — deeper cuts affect staffed hours.
Branch closure is a multi-year Council decision, not an automatic slider result.
Fewer story times, workforce programs, and community events.
Similar to incremental reductions Mesa made in past budget cycles.
Matches real FY 2025/26 cuts: 1.0 FTE pages, security, VR programming.
Minimal public impact per OMB.
Gateway Library continues as first full-service library since 1995.
More literacy outreach, extended hours, additional digital resources.

library.realWorldExample: FY 2025/26: eliminated 1.0 FTE page positions, Press Reader, and VR programming with minimal impact.

levels.label: Reduced force
levels.description: Fewer patrol officers; longer response times in some districts.

levels.label: Below adopted
levels.description: Reduced overtime, fewer specialty units, slower hiring.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Current budget including RTCC 24/7 expansion and 911 staffing additions.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Additional sworn officers toward Quality of Life cap (120 FTE).

levels.label: Maximum
levels.description: Near Quality of Life sworn cap; requires corresponding funding source.

details: Real FY 2025/26 plan: $5M ongoing reduction over 3 years from overtime and incentives.
Eliminated 4 telecommunicator positions and Downtown Engagement Team positions.
Each ~$185K fully loaded sworn officer includes PSPRS pension (~52–59% employer rate).
Radios, vehicles, and body-worn cameras come from separate fleet and lifecycle budgets.
Reduced overtime and delayed specialty program expansion.
RTCC operating 24/7 with added sergeant oversight.
853 sworn officers serve Mesa (~16–18 per 10,000 residents).
Quality of Life fund supports up to 120 sworn FTE.
New officers need 6–12 months academy plus field training.
Equipment: patrol vehicle ~$60K+ amortized, radio, vest, body camera from fleet/tech budgets.

police.realWorldExample: Quality of Life Sales Tax funds up to 120 sworn Police FTE per ballot language.

levels.label: Critical
levels.description: Station brownouts, longer emergency response times.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Delayed academy classes, fewer ambulances.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Stations 223 & 224 under construction; cancer screening program continues.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Faster response in growth areas; additional transport units.

levels.label: Maximum
levels.description: Toward Quality of Life cap of 65 sworn FTE from that fund.

details: Real FY 2025/26: $3M ongoing reduction — 8 Medical Response Unit positions not funded.
Vacant fire pre-recruit position removed.
8 new fire stations added in 20 years; Stations 223 (Lehi) and 224 (Hawes Crossing) building now.
Slower medic academies; deferred apparatus replacement.
Cancer screening on 3-year schedule for sworn members.
Northeast Public Safety Facility opening with Fire Station 222.
Additional ambulances for Transport Services.
Quality of Life supports up to 65 sworn MFMD FTE.

fire-medical.realWorldExample: Ambulance provider exit March 2025 led Mesa to expand citywide transport with 59 new positions.

levels.label: Minimal
levels.description: Pool closures, reduced maintenance, fewer programs.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Fewer recreation programs; deferred park maintenance.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Current parks, pools, and recreation programming.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: More aquatics and recreation staff; improved park amenities.

levels.label: Expanded
levels.description: Major program expansion (many capital projects are CIP/GO bond — see v2).

details: Real FY 2025/26: Fremont Pool closes after summer season.
Mesa Pow Wow city funding eliminated.
Vacant Parks Maintenance Worker position cut.
Fewer camps, sports leagues, and community events.
22.1 new aquatics FTE for lifeguard coverage per audit recommendations.
Eastmark Great Park Phase 5 in CIP (not adjustable here).
Extended pool seasons, additional recreation coordinators.

parks-recreation.realWorldExample: FY 2025/26 added 22.1 aquatics FTE offset by fee revenue increases.

levels.label: Deferred
levels.description: Critical backlog growth; pothole response only.

levels.label: Behind schedule
levels.description: Maintenance falls behind LSST ballot expectations.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Street program within available HURF+LSST resources.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Accelerated pavement preservation and bike/ped projects.

levels.label: Maximum
levels.description: Full program plus reserve drawdown for catch-up.

details: HURF diversions historically caused maintenance to fall behind LSST schedule.
Combined HURF+LSST reserve ~$86M FY 2025/26 — not freely transferable to other services.
Cannot fund libraries or police from these dollars.
Broadway, Val Vista, Ray/Ellsworth projects funded via GO bonds (CIP module v2).
More pavement preservation, signals, and bike/ped infrastructure.

streets-hurf-lsst.realWorldExample: State HURF volatility and recession impacts delayed street maintenance vs. LSST voter expectations.

levels.label: Minimal
levels.description: Severe frequency cuts; paratransit wait times increase.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Fewer routes and reduced peak service.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Current Valley Metro and RPTA contracted service levels.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: More frequency on key corridors.

levels.label: Expanded
levels.description: Major service expansion (requires sustained GF transfer).

details: General Fund transfer to Transit Fund: $26.9M FY 2025/26 (up from $21.8M).
Cuts affect fixed-route frequency and Ride Choice paratransit.
LTAF and transit revenues restricted to transit use.
Contract cost increases $2.9M from labor and maintenance.
Higher frequency requires increased GF subsidy or fare revenue.

transit-operations.realWorldExample: Transit costs exceed fare + state revenue; General Fund covers the gap.

levels.label: At risk
levels.description: Deferred maintenance on treatment plants and lines.

levels.label: Reduced investment
levels.description: Slower growth capacity for southeast Mesa.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Current water/wastewater operations and joint venture participation.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Accelerated infrastructure for growth areas.

levels.label: Maximum
levels.description: Full capacity expansion (Greenfield WRP, Val Vista, 91st Ave plants).

details: Val Vista plant produces ~35% of Mesa water; 91st Ave wastewater share significant.
Southeast Mesa growth demands new infrastructure.
Rate revenue must cover enterprise costs — not general fund discretionary.
Chemical and electric cost pressures in FY 2025/26.
Lead/Copper Rule compliance, SCIF interconnect planning investments.

utilities-water.realWorldExample: Utility operating revenues ~$492M FY 2025/26; enterprise separate from General Fund.

levels.label: Low transfer
levels.description: Minimal contribution to General Government; lower rates possible.

levels.label: Below cap
levels.description: Reduced transfer below $147M adopted.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: $147M transfer (30% cap framework: 25% PS + 5% general).

levels.label: Higher transfer
levels.description: Approaching 30% cap — supports police, fire, libraries.

levels.label: At cap
levels.description: Maximum legal transfer to General Government.

headlines: Lower utility contribution to city services
details: Police, fire, libraries receive significant funding via utility transfer.
Council resolution caps total transfer at 30% of utility operating revenues.
Lower transfer may require higher rates OR General Fund cuts elsewhere.
$147M budgeted FY 2025/26, up 5.3% from prior year.
Cannot exceed 30% without Council policy change.
Trade-off: rates vs. general services funding.

utilities-energy.realWorldExample: Utility contribution is Mesa's method of funding police, fire, libraries, and parks from enterprise revenues.

levels.label: Backlogged
levels.description: Long delays for hearings, arraignments, and warrant processing.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Slower case flow; fewer diversion and compliance programs.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Current court operations handling traffic, misdemeanor, and code cases.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Added diversion programs and faster case resolution.

levels.label: Expanded
levels.description: Specialty courts and expanded compliance services.

details: Municipal Court is constitutionally required — cases must still be heard.
Deep cuts slow arraignments, warrant processing, and fine/restitution collection.
Reduced staff can increase failure-to-appear rates and downstream costs.
Handles traffic, misdemeanor, and city code cases with current staffing.
Expanded diversion and compliance programs; shorter wait times.

municipal-court.realWorldExample: Municipal Court is a mandated function; staffing drives how quickly justice is delivered.

levels.label: Minimal
levels.description: Sharply reduced exhibitions, classes, and community events.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Fewer performances and arts-education offerings.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Mesa Arts Center performances, classes, and museum programming.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: More performances, exhibitions, and outreach.

levels.label: Expanded
levels.description: Major festival and education expansion.

details: Most arts funding flows through the Quality of Life Sales Tax — the General Fund share is small but supports operations and matching.
Cuts reduce classes, exhibitions, and community events at Mesa Arts Center.
Mesa Arts Center is the largest arts campus in Arizona.
Expanded performances, exhibitions, and youth arts education.

arts-culture.realWorldExample: Quality of Life Sales Tax is the primary funding source for Mesa arts and culture.

levels.label: Minimal
levels.description: Sharp reductions to housing, human services, and outreach.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Fewer housing-assistance and senior/family programs.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Current housing, human services, and community outreach.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Expanded homelessness response and human services.

levels.label: Expanded
levels.description: Major program expansion across community needs.

details: Affects housing assistance, homelessness response, and senior/family programs.
Many programs leverage federal grants — local cuts can reduce matching dollars.
Housing, human services, and neighborhood outreach at current levels.
More housing navigation, homelessness response, and outreach capacity.

community-services.realWorldExample: Community Services blends General Fund dollars with federal housing and human-services grants.

levels.label: Slow
levels.description: Long permit and inspection backlogs stall construction.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Slower plan review and inspections.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Current permitting, plan review, and inspection service.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Faster turnaround and expanded online services.

levels.label: Expanded
levels.description: Same-week review for most projects.

details: Development Services is largely fee-supported — cutting it reduces review capacity and delays projects.
Permit delays slow housing, business openings, and the tax base they create.
Plan review, permitting, and inspections at current turnaround times.
Quicker permits support housing and economic growth.

development-services.realWorldExample: Development Services is mostly funded by the permit and review fees it charges.

levels.label: Critical
levels.description: Longer ambulance response and transport times.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Fewer transport units in service.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Citywide EMS transport program (expanded 2025).

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Additional transport units and faster response.

levels.label: Maximum
levels.description: Full coverage with surge capacity.

details: Transport fees recover most program cost — cutting it reduces crews and units.
Mesa expanded citywide transport with 59 positions after a private provider exited in 2025.
Self-sustaining transport program serving the whole city.
More ambulances and crews reduce response and transport times.

ambulance-transport.realWorldExample: Mesa built citywide ambulance transport in 2025 after a private provider exited.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Less frequent collection; recycling scaled back.

levels.label: Below adopted
levels.description: Service or diversion reductions.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Weekly trash, recycling, and bulk/green-waste service.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Expanded diversion and recycling programs.

levels.label: Maximum
levels.description: Highest diversion and convenience service.

details: Solid waste is rate-funded — costs must be recovered from customers, not the General Fund.
Deep cuts could reduce collection frequency or recycling/diversion programs.
Weekly collection plus bulk and green-waste programs.
More recycling and diversion reduces landfill use.

utilities-solid-waste.realWorldExample: Solid Waste is an enterprise service funded entirely by customer rates.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Fewer sworn FTE funded toward the voter-approved caps.

levels.label: Below adopted
levels.description: Slower hiring toward the caps.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Funds up to 120 sworn Police and 65 sworn Fire FTE.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Closer to the sworn FTE caps.

levels.label: At cap
levels.description: Maximum sworn FTE allowed under ballot language.

details: These dollars are dedicated by voters for public safety and quality-of-life — they cannot be redirected to other services.
The tax funds up to 120 sworn Police and 65 sworn Fire FTE plus arts/culture and parks.
Supports sworn public-safety FTE and quality-of-life programs.
Additional sworn FTE toward the voter-approved caps.

quality-of-life-tax.realWorldExample: The Quality of Life Sales Tax funds up to 120 Police and 65 Fire sworn FTE by ballot language.

levels.label: Breakdown
levels.description: Hiring stalls; benefits and workers' comp support lapse.

levels.label: Strained
levels.description: Slower recruiting and benefits administration.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Personnel, Safety/Workers' Comp, and Benefits Administration.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Faster hiring and expanded safety/wellness programs.

levels.label: Robust
levels.description: Strong recruiting, retention, and safety capacity.

headlines: Hiring and benefits administration break down
details: HR runs recruiting, classification & pay, workers' comp safety, and employee/retiree benefits for thousands of staff.
Zeroing or gutting HR halts hiring across every department and disrupts payroll and benefits — you can't run a city without it.
Workers' comp and safety functions reduce costly injuries and liability.
Three divisions: Personnel, Safety Services, and Employee Benefits Administration.
Faster recruiting and expanded workplace safety/wellness.

human-resources.realWorldExample: HR's three divisions keep every other department staffed, paid, and insured.

levels.label: At risk
levels.description: Cybersecurity gaps and outages threaten core systems.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Deferred upgrades; slower support.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Networks, cybersecurity, applications, and data systems.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Modernization and stronger cybersecurity.

levels.label: Leading
levels.description: Resilient, modern technology across the city.

headlines: Cybersecurity and core systems at risk
details: Innovation & Technology runs the networks, security, and applications every department depends on — from 911 dispatch systems to utility billing.
Deep cuts risk outages, security breaches, and failed systems citywide.
Maintains networks, cybersecurity, and enterprise applications.
Stronger cybersecurity and modernized systems.

it-innovation.realWorldExample: IT underpins public safety dispatch, utility billing, permitting, and every digital service.

levels.label: At risk
levels.description: Payroll, purchasing, and audit controls strained.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Slower procurement and billing.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Accounting, payroll, purchasing, treasury, billing.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Faster procurement and stronger controls.

levels.label: Robust
levels.description: Strong financial stewardship and transparency.

details: Financial Services runs accounting, payroll, purchasing, treasury, and utility billing.
Weakening it risks late payments, weaker internal controls, and audit findings that can raise borrowing costs.
Core accounting, payroll, purchasing, and billing operations.
Faster procurement and enhanced transparency.

financial-services.realWorldExample: Strong financial controls protect Mesa's credit rating and lower borrowing costs.

levels.label: At risk
levels.description: Legal review and prosecution capacity sharply reduced.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Slower contract review; outside-counsel costs rise.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Legal counsel, prosecution, and contract/risk review.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Faster legal review and reduced litigation risk.

levels.label: Robust
levels.description: Comprehensive legal support and risk management.

details: The City Attorney provides counsel, prosecutes city cases, and reviews every contract.
Cuts increase litigation risk and force costlier outside counsel.
Counsel, prosecution, and contract review at current capacity.
Faster review and reduced litigation exposure.

city-attorney.realWorldExample: Every city contract and prosecution runs through the City Attorney's office.

levels.label: Stalled
levels.description: Capital projects delayed without design/management staff.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Slower project delivery.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Design, survey, and construction management.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Faster delivery of streets and utility projects.

levels.label: Accelerated
levels.description: Full capital delivery capacity.

details: Engineering designs and manages street, utility, and facility projects — including voter-approved bond work.
Without staff, projects stall even when construction dollars are available.
Design, survey, and construction management at current capacity.
Accelerated street and utility capital projects.

engineering.realWorldExample: Engineering turns bond and CIP dollars into finished streets and utilities.

levels.label: Deferred
levels.description: Building maintenance backlog; HVAC and safety issues grow.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Slower repairs and upkeep.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Upkeep of city buildings and public facilities.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Proactive maintenance and energy upgrades.

levels.label: Optimized
levels.description: Modern, well-maintained facilities.

details: Facilities maintains city buildings, HVAC, and public spaces (libraries, courts, public-safety stations).
Deferring maintenance accumulates costlier emergency repairs and safety risks.
Maintenance of city buildings and public facilities.
Preventive maintenance and energy-efficiency upgrades.

facilities.realWorldExample: Facilities keeps the buildings that house police, fire, courts, and libraries operating.

levels.label: Minimal
levels.description: Little business attraction/retention activity.

levels.label: Reduced
levels.description: Fewer redevelopment and recruitment efforts.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Business attraction/retention and redevelopment.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: More aggressive recruitment and site readiness.

levels.label: Expanded
levels.description: Major employment and tax-base growth efforts.

details: Economic Development recruits and retains employers and supports redevelopment that grows the sales-tax base.
Cutting it can slow the revenue growth that funds every other service.
Business attraction, retention, and redevelopment at current capacity.
More employer recruitment and site development.

economic-development.realWorldExample: A growing employment base expands the sales-tax revenue that funds city services.

levels.label: Breakdown
levels.description: Core administrative functions fail or stall.

levels.label: Strained
levels.description: Slower internal support across departments.

levels.label: Adopted
levels.description: Citywide leadership, clerk, audit, and centralized costs.

levels.label: Enhanced
levels.description: Stronger oversight and support capacity.

levels.label: Robust
levels.description: Highly effective central administration.

details: These functions (City Manager, Clerk, Auditor, Budget Office, contingencies) keep the organization running and accountable.
Contingency reserves also absorb emergencies — cutting them raises fiscal risk.
Leadership, records, audit, budgeting, and centralized costs.
More capacity for audit, budgeting, and emergency contingencies.

internal-services.realWorldExample: Central administration provides the oversight and contingency reserves a $2B organization needs.
